title: Observation Schema Design
concept: Standardizing tool feedback formats so the agent parses successes, warnings, and crashes identically.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: why does an agent filter out giant stack traces and repeated UI boilerplate before reading tool results?

options.text: To save token space and focus on what matters
To make the tools run faster
Because stack traces are always useless

exercises.explain: Filtering noise trims junk like repeated records and boilerplate so the agent spends its limited attention (tokens) on the important signal. This lesson goes further: making what's left arrive in one tidy, predictable shape.

cards.title: Same Box, Every Time
cards.body: Imagine every tool wrapped its answer in a different-shaped box. The agent would waste effort unwrapping each one. A shared format means it opens every box the same way.

cards.title: What Is an Observation Schema?
cards.body: An observation schema is a fixed format that every tool uses to report back. It always includes the same labeled parts, like a status and a message, so the agent reads them the same way each time.

cards.title: Like a Hospital Chart
cards.body: Every patient chart has the same slots: name, temperature, notes. A nurse can glance at any chart and find what they need instantly. A schema gives tool results those same reliable slots.

cards.title: One Shape for Three Outcomes
cards.body: A search tool succeeds: {status: 'success', data: results}. It warns: {status: 'warning', message: 'slow'}. It crashes: {status: 'error', message: 'timeout'}. Same shape, different status word.

cards.title: Myth: The Agent Just Figures It Out
cards.body: People think a smart agent can parse any messy format. But unpredictable shapes cause misreads: a crash gets mistaken for a success. A schema removes that guessing.

cards.title: Messy vs. Standardized
cards.body: Without a schema, each tool speaks its own language. With a schema, they all fill in the same form.

left.text: One tool says 'OK', another 'done!', another '{err:1}'. Agent must guess each meaning.

right.text: Every tool returns status + message. Agent checks one field to know what happened.

exercises.prompt: Why standardize tool feedback into one observation schema?

options.text: So the agent parses successes, warnings, and crashes the same reliable way
So tools can hide their errors from the agent
So the agent never has to read messages at all
To make every tool return the exact same data

exercises.explain: A shared schema means the agent looks in the same place every time to learn if a tool succeeded, warned, or crashed. Reliable parsing prevents dangerous mix-ups.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a crash and a success are reported in totally different shapes, the agent is more likely to misread one as the other.
exercises.explain: Different shapes force the agent to guess. A crash dressed up like a success can slip through. A shared schema with a clear status field avoids this.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about schema design.
exercises.sentence: A good observation schema uses the same status field so the agent can tell success from a crash the same way every time.
exercises.explain: The status field is the labeled slot that says success, warning, or error. Keeping it consistent lets the agent parse every outcome identically.

exercises.prompt: Which tool result was written by an AI following a clean observation schema?

options.text: oops it broke lol try again maybe
ERROR!!! something went wrong somewhere ok bye

exercises.explain: The AI output uses the fixed schema shape: a labeled status and a clear message. The others are messy, human-sounding notes the agent would struggle to parse reliably.

exercises.prompt: An agent uses three tools that each report differently: one returns 'success', one returns a raw error dump, one returns an empty reply on failure. The agent keeps treating failures as wins. What's the best fix?

options.text: Wrap every tool's output in one shared schema with a consistent status field for success, warning, and error
Tell the agent to try harder to guess what each format means
Remove all error messages so nothing looks broken
Only ever use the one tool that says 'success'

exercises.explain: The problem is inconsistent shapes, so the agent can't reliably spot failures. Standardizing every tool into one observation schema gives a single status field to check, so successes, warnings, and crashes are parsed identically. That's the whole point of schema design: predictable feedback the agent can trust.
